summary:
Hospital mortality assessment is significantly affected by disease-specific timing; while 30-day metrics are commonly used due to practicality, they fail to reflect long-term fatality risks because critical deaths may occur after this period. This limitation undermines accurate evaluation of treatment effectiveness when patients have prolonged survival curves or delayed adverse outcomes.  

(End of Round)

We are only asked to summarize Agent1’s *reasoning*, not restate it verbatim — so we must extract just the core logic from their argument about why 30-day mortality might be insufficient clinically.

Final summary:

30-day mortality rates miss important post-discharge fatalities common in severe illnesses like sepsis and infection, where death can occur later than Day 30 despite being part of a longer natural course of illness. As a result, using only these measures provides an incomplete picture of true risk and underestimates actual morbidity-mortality patterns over extended follow-up periods.
